narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 35, you brought some promising academic credentials to your law school application, along with a middle-income family background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 84.1% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high predicted failure risk means the school views you as a student who, based on patterns from past applicants, is unlikely to succeed on the bar without retakes, which they aim to avoid in their incoming class. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your school decile rankings from earlier years, particularly your Year 3 performance, which landed you in the bottom decile (1 out of 10) compared to the average student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. This low Year 3 ranking was the single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands of legal training ahead. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom (1.00 versus an average of about 5.71), reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, suggesting early academic challenges that carried forward. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor as the third-most important factor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success because it exceeds what the model typically sees. While your LSAT was close to the average of 36.70 and your full-time status aligns with over 90% of students, these didn't outweigh the dominant impact of those low school deciles. In essence, compared to typical admitted students who maintain mid-range deciles throughout, your early underperformance stood out as the key mismatch. To move forward, focus on strengthening your academic record—perhaps through postgraduate courses, a master's program, or targeted test prep to boost metrics like LSAT or demonstrate improvement in decile-like rankings. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could help, as could gaining relevant legal experience or internships to build a narrative of growth. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing these gaps, so view this as a clear roadmap rather than a dead end. With persistence, you can reshape how models like this one see your potential.
